title: Court eyes earlier ASIC intervention after MIS failures
full_text:
In her first parliamentary committee address, ASIC chair Sarah Court provided an update on the regulator’s MIS-related efforts, as well as responding to arguments that it is too slow to act on misconduct in the sector.
Managed investment schemes (MISs) have been in both the regulator’s and the government’s spotlight of late due large-scale failures of some such funds which have led to blowout of the Compensation Scheme of Last Resort (CSLR), with a particular focus on the Shield and First Guardian fund collapses.
This has seen ASIC cast a sharper eye on MIS with the backing of government support.
In her opening statement to the Senate at the Economic Legislation Committee on Friday 5 June, Court provided an update on the state of ASIC’s work to improve the supervision of MISs.
“The Government is investing to strengthen oversight of managed investment schemes and that measure will help improve ASIC’s digital capability and help our tools existing non-ASIC government data and enhance our supervision of this sector,” Court told the Senate.
“While we feel there is no ‘silver bullet’ to the complex issues around managed investment schemes, having better access to data will help us respond to existing and emerging threats, including those highlighted by the First Guardian and Shield matters.”
Just last month, the regulator said it would be reviewing the financial reports of MISs, as well as listed and unlisted companies and registrable superannuation entities (RSEs) as part of its priorities for the 2026-27 financial year.
A selection of MISs will also be included in ASIC’s review of 25 audit files, alongside listed and unlisted companies and RSEs with files being pulled from a variety of sources.
Financial Services Minister Daniel Mulino explained that the government is supporting the greater oversight of MIs by allocating funds to the regulator in the latest federal budget with the aim of expanding ASIC’s ability to collect data on flows in MISs as a measure to prevent large-scale collapses.
Mulino told the Stockbrokers and Investment Advisers Association (SIAA) conference in Melbourne last month: “MISs have a critical role as a collective investment vehicle but they can be problematic for retail investors if they are not transparent or clear or where they lack sufficient diversification.
“We are looking at progressive reforms to strengthen the governance and accountability and regulation of MIS alongside enhancing the supervisory capabilities of ASIC. This is about having sensible guardrails without impeding the contribution MISs have made to our capital markets and economy.”
ASIC inaction
While there is heavy emphasis on better equipping ASIC to improve its supervision of MISs, as well as the financial services sector more broadly, the regulator has often faced criticism for being slow to detect and act in matters that – with the benefit of hindsight – should or could have been stopped sooner.
This argument has been a consistent feature of the Shield and First Guardian debate given ASIC only launched its formal investigation and issued stop orders for the funds in early 2024 while its understood that red flags had been raised with the regulator throughout 2023, during which time much of the harm was caused.
Senator Andrew Bragg raised these concerns again on Friday, particularly focusing on ASICs shortcomings when it comes to interfering with those who carrying on financial services businesses under the pretence of being registered financial services firms or holders of an Australian Financial Service license (AFSL).
“We come and come to this bush capital, we make these laws. We say, ‘Here’s what you’ve got to do to be a financial licensed business’. And over here are all these crooks and shonks setting up fake businesses, and frankly, you’ve got to find a better way to close them down, because too many people are being hurt,” Bragg said.
“Then we’ve got to go through all these different convoluted processes to try and help them. It chews up your resources, and when people are going through the trouble of trying to apply for defective administration cases and the like, it’s obviously very taxing for them.
“I’d be much better if ASIC could give evidence in the future about how it’s proposing to close down people who claim to be running a business that is licensed, who actually aren’t.”
Bragg did note that the regulator does seem prepared to take a more proactive approach on such issues but stressed the need to more action in addressing them earlier.
“It sounds like you’re more open to being more aggressive in the future. I mean, we had Melissa Caddick carrying on a financial services business in Sydney for almost a decade. She was not licensed, she stole someone else’s license. And you’ve got these poor people who think that they’re swinging between the flags, so to speak. It’s a big problem.”
Court argued that the issue being raised is less about enforcement and more about early detection, however, but she said it can be hard to walk to line between proactive action and perceived interference.
“We are deeply concerned that there are Australians that lose money to investment schemes and other opportunities all the time.
“We are very open to any suggestions or information about how we might do that better, but this is a fine line, Senator, because many people make a lot of money and do very well out of these kind of schemes and funds as well, and we have to work out, how quickly does an agency like ASIC intervene?
“Sometimes when we intervene too early – they’re the matters you don’t hear complaints about – then investors are very concerned with the action that we’ve taken, because they feel that we have shut down an investment opportunity that they were very happy with.”
While she agreed that the points raised by Bragg “are valid”, Court said there are also deeper complexities that the regulator must balance when it comes to taking “disruptive action”.
“I assure you, we are working hard to bring our data together, bring our intelligence together, and yes, take, perhaps, a more interventionist to shut things down at an early stage.
“But I just want to be open that I do have a slight concern that you’ll then start hearing concerns about ASIC being too activist and closing down things that people don’t want us to, so we’re in the middle of a rock and a hard place sometimes, Senator, but we are very much working on it.”
author: Shy-Ann Arkinstall
heading: Regulation
page_title: Court eyes earlier ASIC intervention after MIS failures - Money Management
